---
title: "Scaling on Satisfaction"
subtitle: "Using OpenTelemetry and User Feedback to Drive Canary Deployments"
author:
  - Whitney Lee
  - Thomas Vitale
date: "2026-03-31"
---

## {.center}

<h2 style="font-size: 1.8em; margin-bottom: 0.8em;">What We're Building</h2>

<p style="font-size: 1.3em;">A live feedback loop where<br/>**audience votes** drive infrastructure decisions.</p>

::: {.notes}
Welcome slide. Set context: in this talk, the audience will participate in two live demo rounds. Their votes on AI-generated stories will literally control the infrastructure — Flagger shifts traffic based on which variant gets more thumbs up. Between demos, we pull back the curtain and show how it all works.
:::

# The App {.center}

::: {.notes}
Section divider: Scene 1 — What the audience sees. Ground them in the user experience before any infrastructure talk.
:::

## Scene 1: What You'll See {.center}

::: {.notes}
Scene 1 opening — blank title slide. Pause here before revealing details one at a time.
:::

## Scene 1: What You'll See {.spaced data-transition="none"}

You'll open a URL on your phone

::: {.notes}
First reveal: just the basic action. The audience will open a URL.
:::

## Scene 1: What You'll See {.spaced data-transition="none"}

You'll open a URL on your phone

You'll read a short **AI-generated story** (5 parts)

::: {.notes}
Second reveal: the content. AI-generated story, 5 parts. The story is about 100 words per part, with named characters.
:::

## Scene 1: What You'll See {.spaced data-transition="none"}

You'll open a URL on your phone

You'll read a short **AI-generated story** (5 parts)

After each part, you vote: **thumbs up** or **thumbs down**

::: {.notes}
Third reveal: the interaction. Thumbs up or thumbs down on each part. Simple binary feedback.
:::

## Scene 1: What You'll See {.spaced data-transition="none"}

You'll open a URL on your phone

You'll read a short **AI-generated story** (5 parts)

After each part, you vote: **thumbs up** or **thumbs down**

What you **don't** see...

::: {.notes}
Fourth reveal: the tease. Pause here for dramatic effect before revealing the hidden mechanics.
:::

## Scene 1: What You'll See {.spaced data-transition="none"}

You'll open a URL on your phone

You'll read a short **AI-generated story** (5 parts)

After each part, you vote: **thumbs up** or **thumbs down**

What you **don't** see: **two variants** of the app exist

::: {.notes}
Fifth reveal: two variants exist. Everyone starts on variant A (the boring one). As votes come in, the system decides whether to shift traffic to variant B. The audience doesn't know this is happening.
:::

## Scene 1: What You'll See {.spaced data-transition="none"}

You'll open a URL on your phone

You'll read a short **AI-generated story** (5 parts)

After each part, you vote: **thumbs up** or **thumbs down**

What you **don't** see: **two variants** of the app exist

Your votes **control what happens next**

::: {.notes}
Sixth reveal: the punchline. Their votes aren't just feedback — they're driving infrastructure decisions in real time. The system will shift traffic based on which variant gets more thumbs up. Don't explain the mechanism yet. Let them sit with the mystery before moving to Scene 2.
:::

## Scene 2: Two Apps, One Stage {data-transition="none"}

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 10
flowchart LR
  A["Variant A<br/>story-app-1a"] ~~~ B["Variant B<br/>story-app-1b"]
```

Two Knative Services running **different container images**

Not feature flags — actually different apps

::: {.notes}
Scene 2, step 1: Start simple. Two Knative Services running side by side. Different images, different behavior. The key insight: variant differentiation via container images, not feature flags. Each has a pod label (app: story-app-1a vs story-app-1b) that becomes the OTel service.name via the Instrumentation CR.
:::

## Scene 2: The Audience Connects {data-transition="none"}

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 10
flowchart LR
  audience["Audience"] --> A["Variant A"]
  audience --> B["Variant B"]
```

Each phone is routed to **one variant** — you don't pick

Knative handles the traffic split

::: {.notes}
Scene 2, step 2: The audience connects. Knative routes each request to one of the two services. The audience doesn't choose — they're assigned. This is the A/B test mechanism: different services, random assignment, same URL.
:::

## Scene 2: SpanContext Round-Trip {data-transition="none"}

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 10
flowchart LR
  S["Server"] -->|"story + spanContext"| P["Phone"]
  P -->|"vote + spanContext"| S
```

Server returns **spanContext** with the story — phone sends it **back** with the vote

This links every vote back to the model that generated it

::: {.notes}
Scene 2, step 3: The spanContext round-trip. This is the non-obvious OTel pattern. When the server generates a story, it captures the span context from the generation span and returns it to the client along with the story text. When the user votes, the client sends that span context back. The server uses it to create a linked evaluation span. This means in your traces, you can navigate from "user voted thumbs up on part 3" directly to "this was generated by Sonnet 4 with these parameters."
:::

## Scene 2: Full Architecture {data-transition="none"}

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 10
flowchart TB
  audience["Audience phones"]
  audience --> va["Variant A"]
  audience --> vb["Variant B"]
  va & vb -->|" "| collector[" "]
  collector -->|" "| platform[" "]
  platform -.->|" "| audience
  style collector fill:none,stroke:none
  style platform fill:none,stroke:none
  linkStyle 2 stroke:none
  linkStyle 3 stroke:none
  linkStyle 4 stroke:none
  linkStyle 5 stroke:none
```

For each story part, you might get **A or B** — it can switch mid-story

::: {.notes}
Scene 2, step 4a: Start with just audience and variants. The invisible nodes below keep the layout stable across all 4 slides. The key insight: this isn't a static A/B test. You might get variant A for part 1 and variant B for part 3 — it depends on which variant is winning.
:::

## Scene 2: Full Architecture {data-transition="none"}

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 10
flowchart TB
  audience["Audience phones"]
  audience --> va["Variant A"]
  audience --> vb["Variant B"]
  va & vb -->|"telemetry"| collector["OTel Collector"]
  collector -->|" "| platform[" "]
  platform -.->|" "| audience
  style platform fill:none,stroke:none
  linkStyle 4 stroke:none
  linkStyle 5 stroke:none
```

Both variants send telemetry to the same **OTel Collector**

::: {.notes}
Scene 2, step 4b: Add the collector. Both variants are instrumented with OpenTelemetry. Every vote, every story generation creates spans and span events. They all flow to the same collector, which is how we can compare the two variants.
:::

## Scene 2: Full Architecture {data-transition="none"}

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 10
flowchart TB
  audience["Audience phones"]
  audience --> va["Variant A"]
  audience --> vb["Variant B"]
  va & vb -->|"telemetry"| collector["OTel Collector"]
  collector -->|"metrics"| platform["Platform"]
  platform -.->|" "| audience
  linkStyle 5 stroke:none
```

The collector turns telemetry into **comparable metrics**

::: {.notes}
Scene 2, step 4c: Add the platform. The collector transforms raw spans into metrics — specifically, thumbs up percentage per variant. The platform (Flagger) watches these metrics.
:::

## Scene 2: Full Architecture {data-transition="none"}

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 10
flowchart TB
  audience["Audience phones"]
  audience --> va["Variant A"]
  audience --> vb["Variant B"]
  va & vb -->|"telemetry"| collector["OTel Collector"]
  collector -->|"metrics"| platform["Platform"]
  platform -.->|"roll out winner"| audience
```

The platform uses those metrics to **roll out the more popular variant**

::: {.notes}
Scene 2, step 4d: Close the loop. The platform shifts traffic toward whichever variant has higher satisfaction. This is the feedback loop — audience votes become telemetry, telemetry becomes metrics, metrics drive the rollout, and the audience gets more of the variant they prefer. We'll dig into each of these steps after Round 1.
:::

# Round 1: Live Demo {.center}

::: {.notes}
Section divider: Round 1 Live Demo. Audience experiences the app for the first time.
:::

## Round 1: Let's Try It

You're about to read a short AI-generated story.

After each part, vote: **thumbs up** or **thumbs down**.

That's it. Just read and react.

::: {.notes}
PRESENTER CUE: Keep this simple — don't reveal that there are two variants or what the difference is. The audience should just experience the app naturally. Switch to the Datadog dashboard on screen, THEN reveal the URL. The reveal of dry vs funny comes AFTER voting, in the debrief slide.
:::

## Time to Vote! {.center}

<!-- TODO (M7): Replace with actual audience URL and QR code -->

### Open on your phone:

**[story-app.kubecon.dev](https://story-app.kubecon.dev)**

Read each story part. Vote thumbs up or thumbs down.

::: {.notes}
PRESENTER CUE: Advance story parts from the admin panel as votes come in. Let 2-3 story parts play out (~3-4 minutes). When ready, click through to the dashboard slide.
:::

## [Live Dashboard](https://app.datadoghq.com/dashboard/68y-xeg-j6s?tv_mode=true) {.center}

Watch the votes come in:

**[Open Datadog Dashboard](https://app.datadoghq.com/dashboard/68y-xeg-j6s?tv_mode=true)**

::: {.notes}
PRESENTER CUE: Click the link to open the Datadog dashboard in TV mode. This shows votes per variant and satisfaction rates updating in real time. Stay on this while the audience votes. The dashboard URL is: https://app.datadoghq.com/dashboard/68y-xeg-j6s?tv_mode=true
:::

## Round 1: The Reveal

There were **two versions** of that story.

| | Variant A | Variant B |
|---|---|---|
| **Tone** | Dry, matter-of-fact | Funny, playful |
| **Model** | Sonnet 4 | Sonnet 4 |

::: {.notes}
The big reveal. The audience didn't know there were two variants. Now show the Datadog dashboard — the funnier variant should have higher satisfaction. Point out that the system was already shifting traffic toward the winner while they were voting.
:::

## {.center data-transition="none"}

<h2 style="font-size: 1.6em; margin-bottom: 1em;">Round 1: What Just Happened?</h2>

<p style="font-size: 1.3em; margin-bottom: 1.2em;">The platform **shifted traffic** toward the variant you liked more.</p>

<p style="font-size: 1.3em;">Now let's look at exactly how.</p>

::: {.notes}
Transition to teaching scenes. The audience saw the feedback loop diagram in Scene 2, but now we'll dig into each component: how votes become telemetry, how the collector turns telemetry into metrics, and how Flagger uses those metrics to shift traffic.
:::

# How It Works {.center}

::: {.notes}
Section divider: Teaching scenes. Now we explain the infrastructure behind what they just experienced.
:::

## Scene 3: How a Vote Becomes Data

<!-- TODO (M5): Incremental span hierarchy diagram -->
<!-- Build 1: User taps thumbs up → app creates span: evaluate UserSatisfaction -->
<!-- Build 2: Span event: gen_ai.evaluation.result with attributes -->
<!-- Build 3: Link back to generation span via gen_ai.response.id -->

::: {.notes}
Scene 3: The Telemetry.
Introduce OpenTelemetry and gen_ai semantic conventions. Key teaching points: span events (not just spans), gen_ai semantic conventions, linking evaluation to generation — the OTel-native way to capture user feedback on AI output.
Incremental build: (1) user taps thumbs up, (2) app creates evaluate UserSatisfaction span, (3) span event gen_ai.evaluation.result with attributes (score.label, score.value, story.part), (4) link back to generation span via gen_ai.response.id.
:::

## Scene 4: From Events to Metrics

<!-- TODO (M5): Incremental collector pipeline diagram -->
<!-- Build 1: Traces arrive via OTLP -->
<!-- Build 2: Transform processor promotes span event attributes to parent span -->
<!-- Build 3: Spanmetrics connector converts spans to gen_ai_calls_total counter -->
<!-- Build 4: Fork to Prometheus (in-cluster) + Datadog (dashboards) -->

::: {.notes}
Scene 4: The Collector — From Events to Metrics.
The OTel Collector transforms raw telemetry into actionable metrics. Key teaching points: the transform "promotion" trick (spanmetrics reads span attributes, not events), spanmetrics as the bridge between traces and metrics, dual export decouples in-cluster decisions from visualization.
Incremental build: (1) OTLP receiver, (2) transform processor, (3) spanmetrics connector → gen_ai_calls_total with dimensions, (4) fork to Prometheus exporter + Datadog exporter.
:::

## Scene 5: Flagger's Canary Logic

<!-- TODO (M6): Incremental Flagger decision loop diagram -->
<!-- Build 1: Prometheus scrapes gen_ai_calls_total every 3s -->
<!-- Build 2: Flagger runs PromQL: (B thumbs_up %) - (A thumbs_up %) -->
<!-- Build 3: Delta >= 5pp for 3 consecutive checks → advance traffic weight by 10% -->
<!-- Build 4: Traffic split progression: 90/10 → 80/20 → ... → 0/100 -->
<!-- Build 5: Delta drops below threshold → rollback to 100% A -->

::: {.notes}
Scene 5: The Decision — Flagger's Canary Logic.
Show how metrics drive automated rollout decisions. Key teaching point: threshold is relative (B must be 5 percentage points better than A), not absolute. The system promotes the variant that makes users happier.
Incremental build: (1) Prometheus scrapes collector, (2) Flagger runs PromQL query, (3) threshold check, (4) traffic split progression, (5) rollback if delta drops.
:::

# Round 2: Live Demo {.center}

::: {.notes}
Section divider: Round 2 Live Demo. Now the audience understands the infrastructure — their votes have new meaning.
:::

## Time to Vote — Again!

<!-- TODO (M4): Same structure as Round 1 but with Round 2 context -->
<!-- TODO (M4): Datadog dashboard + Flagger canary progression view -->

::: {.notes}
Round 2 demo slide.
Variants: 2a (Haiku 4.5, cheap) vs 2b (Opus 4.6, expensive). Same audience interaction, but now they understand the infrastructure. Expected outcome: genuinely unknown — the expensive model might not win. That's the point. Duration: ~3-4 minutes. Dramatic tension: the audience now knows their votes are controlling the rollout, and the outcome is live, not scripted.
PRESENTER CUE: Emphasize that this outcome is NOT predetermined.
:::

## Round 2: What Happened

<!-- TODO (M4): Results, canary progression, which model won -->

::: {.notes}
Debrief Round 2. Show Datadog dashboard + Flagger canary progression. Teaching point: this is how a company decides whether spending more on a premium model is worthwhile. Don't assume the expensive option is better — instrument it, measure real user satisfaction, and let the data decide. The infrastructure doesn't care about price tags; it cares about thumbs up.
:::

# The Full Loop {.center}

::: {.notes}
Section divider: Scene 6 — Closing the feedback loop. Tie everything together.
:::

## Scene 6: Closing the Feedback Loop

<!-- TODO (M6): Full architecture diagram with the feedback loop highlighted -->
<!-- Complete pipeline: Audience votes → App span events → Collector transforms → Prometheus metrics → Flagger queries → Knative traffic split → Audience gets more of the better variant → More votes → ... -->

::: {.notes}
Scene 6: The Full Loop.
Show the complete cycle — audience votes are literally controlling the infrastructure. This is a closed-loop system. Subjective user experience (thumbs up/down) drives infrastructure decisions. This is what "scaling on satisfaction" means.
:::

## What We Built Together

- **OpenTelemetry** gen_ai semantic conventions capture user satisfaction
- **OTel Collector** transforms span events into actionable metrics
- **Flagger** uses those metrics to make canary deployment decisions
- **You** drove the infrastructure with your votes

::: {.notes}
Wrap-up slide. Recap the key technologies and the audience's role. Emphasize: this isn't hypothetical — they just did it, live.
:::

## Thank You

Whitney Lee · Thomas Vitale

<!-- TODO (M7): Links, QR codes, social handles, repo URLs -->

::: {.notes}
Closing slide. Links to repos, slides, social handles. Thank the audience for participating.
:::
